title: iPhone 13 Pro vs. iPhone 17 Pro: Lohnt sich das Upgrade?
transcript: Three weeks ago I switched from an iPhone 13 Pro to an iPhone 17 Pro and compared the two smartphones in detail.  This video will show you in which areas they differ significantly and where they are more similar than you might think. I test all the important aspects like the cameras, performance, and battery life, and then I'll tell you which iPhone I would currently recommend.  I hope you have lots of fun.  Let's go. I am currently giving away 42 of Trackard's rechargeable Airtch cards, which can even ring.  The competition runs until October 31, 2025, and to participate, simply write a comment below this video. Subscribing to my channel and liking the video is also not a bad idea.  Also, feel free to check out Tracker's website, which I've linked in the info box.  And now we begin.  The iPhone 17 Pro comes with a significantly redesigned look that is clearly different from previous generations. While the 13 Pro and the 14 Pro still use a stainless steel frame, Apple uses an aluminum unibody design for the first time in the 17 Pro .  Opinions definitely differ on this. Some find the combination of the thermally conductive aluminum and the integrated vapor chamber ingenious. Others want the more scratch-resistant titanium from the 15 and 16 Pro back, which was only used in the iPhone Air this year.  The front of the 17 Pro is made of the new Ceramic Shield 2, which is significantly more shatter-resistant and scratch-resistant than the Ceramic Shield 1 found in the 13 Pro as well as the 14, 15 and 16 Pro.  At around 206g, the iPhone 17 Pro is also only 2g heavier than the 13 Pro.  Visually, the most noticeable feature is the redesigned camera module , which now protrudes more prominently from the housing.  In terms of color, Apple currently only offers three variants: Silver, Cosmic Orange and Deep Blue, while the 13 Pro was available in Sierra Blue and Silver as well as classic colors like Graffit and Gold.  Although both iPhones are almost the same size, the 17 Pro offers slightly more screen area at 6.3 inches thanks to its thinner display bezels compared to the 13 Pro at 6.1 inches.  The increased brightness is also noticeable .  While the 13 Pro reaches up to 1200 nits, the 17 Pro reaches up to 3000 nits outdoors, making content significantly easier to read in direct sunlight. 1200 nits is also quite bright, and even here you rarely have problems seeing everything clearly.  The iPhone 13 Pro was the first iPhone to feature ProMotion, an adaptive refresh rate of 1 to 120 Hz. The iPhone 17 Pro also has ProMotion and, thanks to Ceramic Shield 2, an anti-reflective coating that makes a difference in some situations compared to previous generations , although not a huge one.  Even though the display of the iPhone 17 Pro is superior here, the differences are not as huge as the price difference might suggest .  Let's take a look at the cameras now. The camera system of the iPhone 17 Pro surpasses the already powerful setup of the iPhone 13 Pro in almost every area.  While the 13 Pro still relies on 3 12 megapixel sensors for wide-angle, ultra-wide-angle and telephoto lenses, the 17 Pro features a new Fusion system in which all three lenses are equipped with 48 megapixel sensors. The main camera sensor on the 17 Pro is slightly larger, which results in even better image quality.  The sensor in the 13 Pro is definitely a solid size for a smartphone.  Both smartphones take decent pictures, although the 17 Pro's are even more detailed thanks to its 48 megapixels.   It becomes especially clear when you zoom in on the images. The difference is also there with the ultra-wide-angle camera, but at first glance it's really only minimal.  Only when you zoom in a bit here do more captured details become visible in the iPhone 17 Pro images. The difference is particularly noticeable with the telephoto zoom.  The iPhone 13 Pro offers triple optical zoom, while the iPhone 17 Pro features a new telephoto lens with quadruple optical zoom.  Additionally, thanks to its 48-megapixel sensor, the iPhone 17 Pro can achieve a so-called lossless zoom of up to eight times by cropping into the central area of ​​the sensor .  Furthermore, the sensor size of the telephoto camera has increased considerably compared to the 16 Pro.  The front camera has also been redesigned.  Instead of 12 megapixels like in the 13 Pro, the 17 Pro now uses a square 18 megapixel sensor with Center Stage function. Furthermore, photos and videos can be taken in landscape format, resulting in a significantly wider-angle image, which is particularly advantageous for selfies with multiple people.  In terms of image quality, the selfies on the 17 Pro are slightly sharper, but even here it's not a huge difference. In the video area, the 17 Pro offers a clear advantage.  Compared to the iPhone 13 Pro, the iPhone 17 Pro supports Lock recording with Apple Logless RAW Openate Genlock and Time Code synchronization features that were previously reserved for professional film cameras .  The video quality from the iPhone 17 Pro is truly incredibly good.  But don't get me wrong, the 13 Pro also takes pretty good videos for a smartphone, but side by side one simply looks much more like a smartphone and the other much more like a professional cinema camera .  To achieve such a look with the be 17 Pro , you often have to spend some time in post-processing .  I also produced a detailed camera review for the iPhone 17 Pro.  I'll definitely link it in the info box.   Feel free to take a look .  And now let's continue with the performance. Thanks to its aluminum unibody design and integrated vapor chamber cooling, the iPhone 17 Pro can dissipate heat more efficiently .  This means that the performance remains more stable even during longer periods of stress than with its predecessors, which tend to start throttling performance earlier under continuous load. While the 13 Pro is still powered by the A15 Bionic , which was manufactured using a 5 nanometer process, Apple uses the new A19 Pro in the 17 Pro, which, like the A18 Pro, is based on a 3 nanometer architecture. Single-core performance has increased by over 50% compared to the 13 Pro, and multi-core performance by over 60%.  Graphics performance has also been noticeably improved.  If we compare the benchmark results of the 17 Pro with the results of the 13 Pro, we see an increase of approximately 125%. This is particularly noticeable in demanding tasks such as gaming, professional video editing, filmmaking with the new professional camera features, or 3D calculations.  Nevertheless, the iPhone 13 Pro still runs super smoothly and is very convenient to use in everyday life.  Apps open faster, in milliseconds, and if there are any stutters, it's usually due to the still buggy iOS 26 and less to the lower peak performance.  So, in terms of performance and especially considering the price-performance ratio, you definitely can't go wrong with the iPhone 13 Pro, even in 2025 .  However, if you really need a lot of performance but don't want to spend quite so much, I recommend looking at a refurbished iPhone 16 Pro, which also has a chip with 3 nanometer architecture and also has very strong performance in both the CPU and GPU areas.  And as just mentioned, let's briefly talk about iOS 26. Opinions on this are at least as divided as they are on the design of the 17 Pro.  The new Liquid Glass look makes everything appear a bit more transparent and smoother.  While this is aesthetically pleasing, it also means that, for example, text is sometimes harder to read and the reflection makes it appear slightly less minimalist .  Furthermore, there are, for example, new lock screen interfaces with dynamic home position and a revised camera app with changed switches. Both smartphones are also IP68 certified, which means they are protected against dust and immersion for up to 30 minutes. Unlike the iPhone 13 Pro, the iPhone 17 Pro features Apple Intelligence, Apple's integrated AI system. In the long term, Apple Intelligence aims to turn the iPhone into a personal assistant that, for example, takes over tasks such as summarizing texts or documents and provides personalized suggestions.  Many functions are also intended to run directly on the device to protect privacy. For many, however, this doesn't play a huge role in their purchase decision for the iPhone 17 Pro, as many of the features are not yet fully available or only usable to a limited extent, if at all.  So, these are the microphones of the two iPhones. You can see if you can notice a difference.  This , as I said, is the iPhone 13 Pro and this is the iPhone 17 Pro.  Exactly.  And now we'll continue with the battery.  While the iPhone 13 Pro manages with a battery of around 3100 mAh, the iPhone 17 Pro has approximately 4000 mAh.  This increase, combined with the more efficient A19 Pro chip and optimized energy management through better heat dissipation, results in a noticeably longer battery life in everyday use.  According to Apple, the iPhone 17 Pro achieves a local video playback time of up to 31 hours, while the 13 Pro manages about 22 hours under the same conditions .  Even in more demanding usage scenarios, such as streaming or high display brightness, the new model lasts noticeably longer. Both iPhones can be charged wirelessly via Maxave or a charging pad. Here, the iPhone 13 Pro achieves up to 15 watts and the iPhone 17 Pro up to 25 watts charging power.  Using a cable, the iPhone 17 Pro can reach up to 50% charge in about 20 minutes.  Provided that a high-performance power supply is used. The iPhone 13 Pro reaches 50% in about 30 minutes.  This one also uses the Lightning connector, while the iPhone 17 Pro uses USB-C.  When buying a refurbished iPhone 13 Pro, you should make sure that the battery is not too worn out.  My recommendation would be at least 85%, even better would be 90% maximum capacity in terms of battery health.  Conclusion: The iPhone 13 Pro is still a very powerful device.  Even today, a refurbished model for under €400 can provide a smartphone that offers solid quality in many areas.  It boasts strong performance, good camera quality, and is the first iPhone with a Pro Motion display.  The iPhone 17 Pro, however, offers noticeable improvements with a powerful A19 Pro chip with 3 nanometer architecture, an improved camera system with professional video features, an improved battery, and the new aluminum unibody design with Ceramic Shield 2 and better heat dissipation.  If you're looking for a sweet spot, you could take a look at the iPhone 15 Pro, which, although it has a chip with 5 nanometer architecture, still has Apple Intelligence and is generally very close to the iPhone 16 Pro.  Feel free to write in the comments which iPhone you would currently buy and what you think of the iPhone 17 Pro .  Please give the video a thumbs up if you liked it.  Subscribe to my channel if you're interested in videos like this. That would make me incredibly happy.  And then I wish you a nice day.  Goodbye, my dears.  Until then, peace.

The iPhone 17 Pro weighs 206g, which is mainly due to significantly fewer components and a much thinner battery . Due to its design, the iPhone Air also has this feature on the back.  I just briefly mentioned the dimensions. Those who prefer a slightly more compact device are better off with the iPhone 17 Pro. Incredibly, both models use Face ID for unlocking, the 3D face unlock that Apple has been offering for years, and it actually works perfectly on both models. However, Apple takes a different approach to the speaker setup, particularly on the iPhone Air, where only a mono speaker is used above the front camera.  The iPhone 17 Pro also features a speaker in the base of the device, which this year has been moved to the left side of the USB-C port. Both of these setups definitely sound good.  The iPhone 17 Pro, due to its two speakers, is also significantly more spacious, and due to the thickness of the casing, also significantly bulkier.  They sound more nuanced and, in my opinion, are 30 to 40% better than those of the iPhone Air. Regarding the displays, I already briefly mentioned it.  6.3 inches in the iPhone 17 Pro, 6.5 inches in the iPhone Air, both with a maximum refresh rate, i.e., how smoothly the image can be displayed, up to 120 Hz and a maximum brightness of 3000 nits in automatic mode.  Everything related to color accuracy, brightness, viewing angle stability, and so on is at an identical and really good level, so both models actually get a point here.  Let's move on to the next two points: performance, and consequently, of course, heat generation and, above all, battery life.  Both models use the A19 Pro chip .  However, the A19 Pro in the iPhone Air, and of course the graphics unit as well, are somewhat less powerful, and theoretically the iPhone Air, especially when under load, will reach its limits more quickly.  Of course, all of this can also be done in various stress tests, and that is just as understandable.  However, I can also tell you that in everyday use, and this will affect 99.5% of all users, it will not be noticeable at all , as it will still be among the most powerful iPhones on the market .  Under full load, I recorded a heat development of approximately 43°C in the camera area of ​​the iPhone Air .  As mentioned, the iPhone 17 Pro uses the slightly more powerful A19 Pro chip .  We have very good heat dissipation here, for which the aluminum housing is of course ideally suited, and under full load I suddenly reached 38°C.  This also clearly demonstrates how well the thermals work and that the Pro model is definitely intended for more performance-oriented users.  Regarding battery life, I got roughly identical results for both models.  Yes, the iPhone 17R has a significantly smaller battery, but the processor is also somewhat less powerful. I had at least 4.5 to 5 hours of screen-on time here .  With the iPhone 17 Pro, it was usually five hours, sometimes even 5.5 hours, although on both devices I always had automatic brightness control set or between 50 and 60% of the maximum brightness .  In the evenings, around 9 pm, I usually still had about 20 to 25% remaining battery capacity in both devices.  When I simply ran everything over Wi-Fi, watched Netflix, manually adjusted the brightness and volume to 50% , I achieved video playback times of between 15 and 16 hours on both devices .  Therefore, regarding performance, and especially battery and charging, I am extremely satisfied with both models, and I am also absolutely surprised by the iPhone Air, so that I would actually award the point to the iPhone Air , since it has a significantly smaller battery.   Let 's talk about the camera, which is actually one of the most important aspects of smartphones these days, and of course also of Apple devices, and therefore also of both models here.  We have one thing in common on the front: the 18 megapixel front camera.  Otherwise, we have a completely different setup, namely the iPhone Air only has one camera.  This is a 48 megapixel main camera, whereas with the Proas we have a 48 megapixel main camera, a corresponding telephoto camera and another 48 megapixel ultra wide-angle camera.  Anyone who is already at the point where they say, " I need macro photography in a smartphone, or ultra- wide-angle photography in a smartphone, or good telephoto capabilities, meaning to bring objects closer, be it building structures in urban areas or even the birds here in autumn in the thorn hedge in the countryside."  He can skip this chapter right now and should go for the iPhone 17 Pro, because the iPhone is unlikely to be able to do all that, or at least not with sufficient power.  There is no macro photography or macro camera in the car, no ultra-wide-angle camera, which is usually also associated with it.  There is a 2x telephoto zoom, but that's a digital crop.  This means that by digitally zooming into the image, this doubled quality is achieved; with the iPhone 17 Pro, up to eight times zoom is possible, and accordingly, up to eight times better quality is achieved.  Furthermore , as mentioned, there is the ultra-wide-angle camera, which is currently among the best on the market, and also a main camera that reproduces very natural photos, just like the one on the iPhone Air, which is also great. Therefore, if we compare the two cameras, the iPhone 17 Pro and the Air, I'm talking about the 48 megapixel main camera here, then you will actually find very good, sometimes even very identical, pictures on both devices. That means the main camera in the iPhone Air is really very good, too. However, I found the front camera on both devices to be very, very similar.  That means I had good color reproduction and a good level of detail.  For me, the images on both smartphones were a bit too soft, which is n't noticeable when comparing them directly, but especially when compared to other devices, such as the Pixel 10 Pro.  They are good to very good, I would say, but in my opinion not as good as on a Samsung smartphone or even the Pixel 10 Pro.  However, the video quality is identically good on both smartphones at 4K and 60 frames per second, so very good even with the iPhone 17 Pro perhaps with slight advantages. The 17 Pro offers significantly greater advantages , especially in that it allows you to record video in Pro mode and also to take photos in RAW, which the iPhone simply doesn't offer.  This is where the difference becomes most apparent.  The ProDell is designed for the professional user.  The Air model is more for users who want a stylish smartphone. I don't really need to elaborate on the software, because one thing should be clear: both devices use iOS 26 .  And anyone who has seen my videos about the iPhone Pro or the iPhone 17 Pro will have noticed that I haven't always spoken positively about them, because for me these iOS 26 versions are the most unfinished versions that Apple has ever released for a new device.  I need to harp on this point again, namely iOS 26 at the current time, because I recently read in comments that other manufacturers aren't any better.  However, I cannot actually recall that any manufacturer selling these kinds of premium devices for over €1200 has ever included as many bugs or crashes in the operating system in the last 5 years as Apple has with the latest iPhone generation.  And I hear that again and again from my circle of friends.   One feature that is definitely worth mentioning is Apple Intelligence, Apple's own AI assistant, which can be activated by holding down the power button and which delivers really great results, especially in collaboration with ChatGPT .  This has been really well solved, although, for example, photo editing on Apple devices doesn't work as well as on Google smartphones or especially on Samsung smartphones.  That brings us to my brief conclusion. Which models are suitable for whom?  The iPhone 17R, no, it's only called the iPhone Air if you're looking for a lightweight and extremely thin device, need a large bright display, but do n't necessarily need the zoom, ultrawide, ultrawide angle, or macro monster in the camera, and the everyday performance of the scaled-down CPU and GPU is definitely sufficient.  Another negative point , at least in my opinion, is the single mono speaker above the display, as it simply cannot convey this spatial sound as well as, for example, the Filter 17 Pro.  The Pro model is really for you if you want maximum performance, better thermals, and of course, a better camera.   In my opinion, both are relatively equal when it comes to displays. However, in my opinion, even the Pro model uses the inferior material, or rather this mix, namely the aluminum combination with Seramic Shield, which makes titanium my first choice for the iPhone Air .  In summary, if you 're looking for a smartphone that's super high-quality and has a Wowtor camera, and you only rarely use the camera, for example to quickly take a snapshot, then the iPhone is definitely the right choice for you.  However, if you say you're a hardcore gamer, you take a lot of photos and you really enjoy doing so, then the iPhone 17 Pro is definitely the better choice for you.  And that's exactly where I end up.  Thank you for watching.  I would be happy if you enjoyed the video and of course if you tune in to future videos.  Until then, take care and bye.

But the biggest leap was actually made by the iPhone. While the Pixel 10 Pro increases by 170 mAh to a total of 4870 mAh , the iPhone 17 Pro gains over 400 mAh, reaching almost 4000 mAh. But wait a second, that's over 20% less.  How can the iPhone compete with that?  The answer: A very efficient chip and excellent adaptation of the software to the hardware. And that's why the iPhone boasts good battery life, as I average 5.5 to 1.5 hours of screen time in everyday use.  The Pixel 10 Pro, on average, lasts for four and a half to five and a half hours.  Both will get you through a day just fine.  Neither of them are real battery monsters, though .  For that, you'd need something like the Pixel Pro XL or iPhone Pro Max. In the Android world, the Pixel 10 Pro achieves a PC battery benchmark that, unfortunately, does n't exist for iPhones.  At just under 13 and a half hours, however, that's a really good result.   This means it lasts longer than a Samsung S25 Plus, but it doesn't come close to a Xiaomi 15. Apple and Google are very similar when it comes to charging.  While the Pixel cable is limited to up to 30 watts, the iPhone draws up to 35 watts and is unusually fast.  This means both devices are fully charged in approximately 80 to 100 minutes , which isn't exactly super fast. But there is some good news regarding wireless charging.  Pixel Snap finally brings magnets to G2 charging.  Just like with the iPhone and Maxf, you can now attach a magnetic puck, a power bank, or other accessories to the back.  And yes, Maxf gadgets are also compatible with the Pixel .  While the iPhone already uses up to 25 watts of LED power, the Pixel only offers 15 watts. Both smartphones come with a triple camera including a periscope telescope, so they have very similar features in this respect as well.  The Pixel features a 50 megapixel main camera, a 48 megapixel ultra-wide-angle camera, and a 48 megapixel telephoto camera with five times optical zoom.  The iPhone features a 48- megapixel main camera, a 48-megapixel ultra-wide camera, and a 48- megapixel telephoto camera with four times optical zoom.  Which of the two takes the better photos?  In daylight, both smartphones deliver very strong results, although with some differences in characteristics.  The iPhone uses a slightly warmer color scheme.  With this classic car, the Apple smartphone can therefore capture the turquoise side more accurately.  The pixel is slightly off here.  This delicious pizza looks even more delicious on the Pixel .  Don't you think so too?  Why the pixel depicts the yellow autumn landscape as if it were currently an ice age is a mystery to me.  Thankfully, that remained the exception.  The iPhone delivers slightly crisper images thanks to its standard 24-megapixel camera .  The difference is only noticeable upon close inspection. However, the close focusing distance is clearly in favor of the Pixel.  It focuses from about 12 cm, while the iPhone only focuses from almost 17 cm .  After that, the iPhone automatically activates macro mode via the ultra-wide camera, which doesn't always look high-quality.  Nevertheless, the ultra-wide-angle cameras score points with their good quality.  Both deliver sharp, unforgiving images with similar color and detail quality. Surprisingly often, the results are almost identical.  In the opposite direction, both smartphones impress with a high-quality 2x zoom through the main camera. The optical zoom via the periscope cameras also delivers sharp and detailed results. The Pixel tends to produce brighter zoomed-in photos, while the iPhone captures slightly more fine detail.  When digitally magnified, it becomes apparent that the eight-fold hybrid image of the iPhone sometimes appears clearer than the ten-fold pixel image. However, the situation changes at around 30x magnification .  The Pixel scores points here with its AI- based Super Resolution Zoom, which retains a surprising amount of detail, especially at 100x zoom, although not always perfectly.  On the iPhone, however, the digital zoom stops at 40x.  For night shots, I prefer the Pixel slightly, as it makes the scene appear brighter overall and brings out more detail from dark areas .  The iPhone, on the other hand, captures the lighting mood in a slightly more atmospheric way.  I am impressed by the macro mode on both the Pixel and the iPhone.  Close-up shots from both artists are exciting and detailed. However, I particularly prefer the pixelated look for flowers, as the texture is finer and the colors are more natural. Regarding portraits, both provide a clean separation between subject and background sharpness.  Personally, I prefer the portraits on the Pixel, even though the iPhone looks more natural with a softer transition at the edges .  In terms of video quality, both are top-level.  The Pixel does n't need to hide from the iPhone here.  The zoom function is smooth and the stabilization is pleasantly gentle. However, they differ significantly in color.  Technically, the Pixel offers a higher level of detail with 8K videos, while the iPhone, with its professional Pro RAW format, is clearly aimed at movies.  Finally, a word about selfies.  Here, the iPhone can of course stand out due to its almost square sensor, which makes it possible to take photos in portrait and landscape format without rotating the smartphone. Compared to the Pixel, iPhone selfies appear somewhat softer and not quite as crisp. In the end, we have two really strong compact smartphones here that have hardly any weaknesses.  High-quality, IP68-rated water-resistant design with super bright 120 Hz OLEDs, powerful triple camera, good battery life and magnetic wireless charging.  The iPhone 17 Pro scores points with its enormous performance, longer battery life despite a smaller battery, and the usual close interconnectivity within the Apple ecosystem.  I personally really like the new design, especially here in orange. For creators, Proest RA Video is a particularly strong argument, and in general, the iPhone is a really good phone for filming.  I also think the flexible front camera with its almost square sensor is really clever. The maximum focus distance of the main camera could be improved, the full-screen brightness is somewhat lower, storage upgrades are extremely expensive, and iOS 26 is still struggling with minor bugs, not to mention the current, still limited, integration of AI features. In comparison, the Pixel 10 Pro features a super bright display and a significantly better focus limit for the main camera.  But in my opinion, its greatest strength is the software. Clean Android 16 with countless useful AI features such as Gemini Live, Live Call Translation or Circle to Search.  8K video capabilities and a good, though not outstanding, battery life are also advantages .  The Tensor G5 chip used may not be in the top league in terms of performance, but it is fast enough for everyday use.  Criticism is leveled at the camera's sometimes inconsistent color calibration and the sluggish UFS 3.1 storage in the base version.  Incomprehensible for a flagship model in this price range. Ultimately, both could be a little faster when charging , but all in all, these are two extremely sophisticated smartphones, each playing to its own strengths .  The iPhone with its performance and ecosystem, the Pixel with its clever AI features.  For true battery monsters, you should opt for the larger Pixel 10 Pro XL or iPhone 17 Pro Max. The Pixel 10 Pro is already available for under €950.  However, I recommend the 256 GB version starting at €1050.  The iPhone 17 Pro, on the other hand, is close to its RRP of €1299.  Let's see when and if it gets cheaper.  Which of the compact Pros would you ultimately choose?   Feel free to write it in the comments.  I'm curious to hear your opinions.  Tune in again soon. It's good.  See you later.  Ciao.
